tasks.prompt: [Warm-up] Run kubectl api-resources to list all resource types registered in your cluster. Read the NAMESPACED, KIND, and APIVERSION columns in the output, then filter to show only resources that belong to the 'apps' API group.
tasks.starter:
# List all API resources
kubectl api-resources

# Filter to the apps group only
kubectl api-resources --api-group=___

# List CustomResourceDefinitions (CRDs) — none yet is fine
kubectl get crd
tasks.solution:
# List all API resources
kubectl api-resources
# Sample output (partial):
# NAME                  SHORTNAMES  APIVERSION         NAMESPACED  KIND
# pods                  po          v1                 true        Pod
# deployments           deploy      apps/v1            true        Deployment
# ...

# Filter to the apps group only
kubectl api-resources --api-group=apps
# Sample output:
# NAME                  SHORTNAMES  APIVERSION  NAMESPACED  KIND
# controllerrevisions               apps/v1     true        ControllerRevision
# daemonsets            ds          apps/v1     true        DaemonSet
# deployments           deploy      apps/v1     true        Deployment
# replicasets           rs          apps/v1     true        ReplicaSet
# statefulsets          sts         apps/v1     true        StatefulSet

# List CRDs (results vary by cluster)
kubectl get crd
# If nothing is installed: No resources found
tasks.explanation: kubectl api-resources lists all resource types registered in the cluster. APIVERSION takes the form group/version (e.g., apps/v1); core-group resources (v1, etc.) show only the version without a group name. Adding a CRD inserts a new entry into this list, and from that point the custom resource is treated exactly like a built-in resource. The --api-group flag lets you filter by group.
hints: If the output of kubectl api-resources is long, pipe it with '| grep apps' to filter.
NAMESPACED: true means the resource is namespace-scoped; false means it is cluster-scoped.
Add -o wide to kubectl api-resources to also see the VERBS column.

tasks.prompt: [Core Practice] Write and register a CRD named 'databases.myapp.example.com' with the following schema: name (string, required), engine (string, default 'postgresql'), and replicas (integer, range 1–5). After registering, confirm it with kubectl get crd, then attempt to create an invalid custom resource with replicas set to 10 to verify that schema validation rejects it.
tasks.starter:
# Start writing database-crd.yaml
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: databases.myapp.example.com
spec:
  group: myapp.example.com
  versions:
    - name: v1
      served: true
      storage: true
      schema:
        openAPIV3Schema:
          type: object
          properties:
            spec:
              type: object
              required:
                - ___          # specify required field
              properties:
                name:
                  type: ___
                engine:
                  type: string
                  default: ___
                replicas:
                  type: integer
                  minimum: ___
                  maximum: ___
  scope: Namespaced
  names:
    plural: databases
    singular: database
    kind: Database
    shortNames:
      - db
tasks.solution:
# database-crd.yaml
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: databases.myapp.example.com
spec:
  group: myapp.example.com
  versions:
    - name: v1
      served: true
      storage: true
      schema:
        openAPIV3Schema:
          type: object
          properties:
            spec:
              type: object
              required:
                - name
              properties:
                name:
                  type: string
                engine:
                  type: string
                  default: postgresql
                replicas:
                  type: integer
                  minimum: 1
                  maximum: 5
  scope: Namespaced
  names:
    plural: databases
    singular: database
    kind: Database
    shortNames:
      - db

---
# Apply it
kubectl apply -f database-crd.yaml
# Output: customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/databases.myapp.example.com created

# Confirm CRD registration
kubectl get crd databases.myapp.example.com
# NAME                           CREATED AT
# databases.myapp.example.com    2024-01-01T00:00:00Z

# Validation test: attempt replicas=10 (out of range)
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: myapp.example.com/v1
kind: Database
metadata:
  name: bad-db
spec:
  name: test
  replicas: 10
EOF
# Error example:
# The Database "bad-db" is invalid:
#   spec.replicas: Invalid value: 10: spec.replicas in body should be less than or equal to 5
tasks.explanation: The spec.versions[].schema.openAPIV3Schema section of a CRD defines the field types and constraints for custom resources. Listing a field name in the required array causes the API server to reject creation attempts that omit it. minimum/maximum validates integer ranges, and default automatically fills in a value when the field is not specified. All of this validation happens at the API server level, so invalid resources are blocked even without a controller.
hints: The CRD's metadata.name must follow the '<plural>.<group>' format. Example: databases.myapp.example.com
Setting scope to Namespaced isolates resources per namespace; Cluster makes them cluster-wide.
After registering a CRD it may take a few seconds to reach the Established state. Check with: kubectl get crd <name> -o yaml | grep -A5 'status:'

tasks.prompt: [Core Practice] Using the Database CRD you registered in the previous step, create two custom resources. The first should have name='prod-db', engine='mysql', and replicas=3. The second should specify only name='dev-db' so you can confirm that the engine default is automatically applied. After creation, query the resources with kubectl get db, kubectl describe db prod-db, and kubectl get db -o yaml.
tasks.starter:
# prod-db custom resource
apiVersion: myapp.example.com/v1
kind: Database
metadata:
  name: prod-db
  namespace: default
spec:
  name: ___
  engine: ___
  replicas: ___

---
# dev-db: omit engine to verify the default behavior
apiVersion: myapp.example.com/v1
kind: Database
metadata:
  name: dev-db
  namespace: default
spec:
  name: ___

# Save to a file and apply
# kubectl apply -f databases.yaml

# Query commands (fill in the blanks and run)
kubectl get ___
kubectl describe ___ prod-db
kubectl get ___ -o yaml
tasks.solution:
# databases.yaml
apiVersion: myapp.example.com/v1
kind: Database
metadata:
  name: prod-db
  namespace: default
spec:
  name: prod-db
  engine: mysql
  replicas: 3

---
apiVersion: myapp.example.com/v1
kind: Database
metadata:
  name: dev-db
  namespace: default
spec:
  name: dev-db

# Apply
kubectl apply -f databases.yaml
# database.myapp.example.com/prod-db created
# database.myapp.example.com/dev-db created

# Query using the shortName 'db'
kubectl get db
# NAME      AGE
# dev-db    5s
# prod-db   5s

# Detailed query
kubectl describe db prod-db

# YAML output (verify the engine default)
kubectl get db dev-db -o yaml
# spec:
#   engine: postgresql   <-- default was automatically applied
#   name: dev-db
tasks.explanation: Once a CRD is registered, custom resources can be managed with the same kubectl get/describe/delete/apply commands as built-in resources (Pod, Deployment, etc.). Defining shortNames (e.g., db) enables abbreviated commands like kubectl get db. The API server persists custom resources to etcd, but this alone does not create an actual database. Reacting to the custom resource and performing real work is the role of a controller (Operator), which is covered in the next lecture.
hints: apiVersion is the CRD's spec.group + '/' + spec.versions[].name. Example: myapp.example.com/v1
Both kubectl get databases and kubectl get db work. shortNames are convenience aliases.
To verify the dev-db engine default, look for 'postgresql' in the spec section of the '-o yaml' output.

tasks.prompt: [Application] Clean up the custom resources you created and delete the CRD. Observe whether deleting the CRD also removes all custom resources that belonged to it. Then confirm that the Database kind has disappeared from kubectl api-resources output.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Optionally delete the custom resources first
kubectl delete db prod-db dev-db

# 2. Delete the CRD
kubectl delete crd ___

# 3. Try querying db resources after deletion (error expected)
kubectl get db

# 4. Confirm it is gone from api-resources
kubectl api-resources | grep ___
tasks.solution:
# 1. Delete custom resources
kubectl delete db prod-db dev-db
# database.myapp.example.com "prod-db" deleted
# database.myapp.example.com "dev-db" deleted

# 2. Delete the CRD
kubectl delete crd databases.myapp.example.com
# customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "databases.myapp.example.com" deleted

# 3. Try querying after deletion
kubectl get db
# error: the server doesn't have a resource type "db"

# 4. Confirm removal from api-resources
kubectl api-resources | grep databases
# (no output — fully removed)

# Note: deleting the CRD first cascades and removes all custom resources belonging to it
# kubectl delete crd databases.myapp.example.com
# (prod-db and dev-db are also deleted)
tasks.explanation: Deleting a CRD cascades and removes all custom resources belonging to that group/version/kind. After deletion, kubectl queries for those resources return an error because the API server no longer recognizes the resource type. Since a CRD is an API extension mechanism, registering a CRD = adding a new API endpoint, and deleting a CRD = removing that endpoint.
hints: It is safer to delete custom resources before deleting the CRD. If finalizers are set, the CRD deletion may block until they are cleared.
kubectl delete crd <name> may complete asynchronously. Use kubectl get crd to confirm it has fully disappeared.
In production, always verify that no Operators or applications depend on a CRD before deleting it.
